video_name: Video_14_Vector Stores in LangChain ｜ Generative AI using LangChain ｜ Video 12 ｜ CampusX.mp3
full_text: Hi guys, my name is Nitesh and you are welcome to my YouTube channel In this video we will continue our Lang chain playlist and we are moving towards making a rag based application and I am teaching you the important components of rag one by one So till now we have read document loaders, we have read tech splitters Now in today's video we will read about vector stores which is a very important component of rag So the plan for this video is that I will first tell you about vector stores I will give you some idea about different types of vector stores Why vector stores are needed, I will explain you this through a real world example and finally how you can use vector stores in a Lang chain we will see that with the help of a code On the whole this video is going to be very important If you want to understand rag well, then please make sure you watch this video end to end Now let's start the video Now guys, before understanding what are vector stores, I want to explain to you why vector stores are needed and to explain this I will take an interesting use case, I will take an example and that example is about movies So suppose you want to make a website where you want to list down every movie of the world like IMDB So essentially you want to make a movie catalog system which will look like this Here the user will come and search any movie and he will get all the information about that movie Now if you think how to make this website then if we talk step by step, then the first step will be that you should have a database in which you should have all the movie's data Right? Means you should have a database like this where you should have all the information related to the movie like movie's ID, movie's name, director's name, actor's name, movie's genre, when it was released, what was the outcome of the movie, hit, flop Apart from this you can put a lot of information here Now where will you get this information from? There are many ways Either you can go to any public API and fetch the data or you can bring the data with the help of web scraping But once you have this database then what you can do is you can make a backend system Let's say using python which will pull the data from this database and send it to your front end and your front end will display that data So roughly speaking this is the way to make this website To have a database, to have a backend, to have a front end Right? Now let's say you have made this website But now slowly you want to bring some improvements in this The first improvement you thought was what if we add a movie recommender system in this website Which basically means if our user is on a particular movie page Let's say he is opening the spiderman page and seeing the information related to spiderman So we will not only show the movie information but at the same time at the bottom of the page we will suggest some other movies which will be very similar to spiderman For example we will list iron man there We will list captain america there Right? So what will be the benefit of this? If this movie recommender system works well then the engagement of the user can increase Now he is watching spiderman While watching he saw iron man Since he is in the same thought process So he will click on iron man and read about iron man Then he will see captain america He will spend more time on your website And in turn you can flip it and show more ads and earn money Right? So this idea came to your mind Now you thought how to make this movie recommender system So a very simple solution can be that you do keyword matching Keyword matching is very simple You have two movies M1 and M2 And you will compare these two on the basis of different parameters Like who is the director of the movie Who is the actor of the movie What genre is the movie And their release date is around If all these keywords match Means the director of two movies is same The actor is same The genre is same This means these two movies are very similar And if the user is watching one movie Then we can recommend him another movie Whereas if the keywords of two movies are very less The director is different, the actor is different, the genre is different It means that these movies are very dissimilar And it will not be beneficial to show them together And by using this simple idea You have made your movie recommender system And guess what This movie recommender system is working well Movies are recommending And users are using this system But if you think carefully Then there is a big flaw in this approach What is that flaw The flaw is that sometimes The recommendations of this particular system Will not be logical For example Suppose I am a user And I have just seen My Name is Khan And I have just seen My Name is Khan I liked this movie And I want to watch more movies like this But when I opened your website The recommendation that I saw The top recommendation for My Name is Khan Was never say goodbye Now if you think If I have just seen My Name is Khan And I want to watch this type of movie Then never say goodbye But if you think this is not a good recommendation Because this movie is completely different It has a different type of story line So why did our system Tell these two movies to be similar The answer is because you are matching the keywords If you talk about these two movies The director of these two movies is the same Karan Johar The lead actor is the same Shah Rukh Khan Both have come in the same time One came in 2006 and one in 2009 And the genre is similar Drama is a common genre So many keywords have matched So our system thought My Name is Khan should be the most similar movie Never say goodbye But is it really similar The answer is no Second flaw Through another example I will tell you Some movies will be similar But our system Will never be able to tell them similar Because there will be no keywords Common between them I don't know if you have seen these two movies or not But in these two movies There is a central character Who is struggling with some disease But at the same time He is brilliant in some other aspect And then his story is told So in that sense these two movies are very similar But if you talk about keywords The keywords are completely different The directors are different The actors are different These movies have come in different time Since the movie is a movie Since the movie is a movie Since the keywords are not matching Our algorithm will never be able to tell them similar In nutshell We can say that the system we have made Is very simple And in some scenarios It will work and give you But most of the times You can't expect quality recommendations from it Because The underlying principle you have used Is very simple Keyword matching And when you understood That your movie recommendation system Is not that great And your user engagement is decreasing Instead of increasing Then you started thinking What is a better approach How can I improve this system Now if you pause the video And think how can we improve this system Then you will get a straight answer In your mind That we have to tell If two movies are similar or not And the best way to tell Is that You can compare The plot of two movies If the story of two movies Is very similar It means that both movies are very similar If the stories are very dissimilar Then the movies are dissimilar So the basic idea is that Rather than matching keywords We should compare The plot of two movies And you liked this idea Now the problem is To execute this idea You should have Every movie's plot Which is not in your database So you worked hard You searched for APIs Or web scraping And you extracted every movie's plot And put it in your database So this is our new database Where our movie's id is This is our 3dids id And in front of it You have put the plot of 3dids Now this plot You can see it as one paragraph But it is actually A complete text of 2000-3000 words And I did this With every movie This is Chennai express This is inception This is S3 So basically I have this new database Where I have every movie's name Id and its plot Now the challenge in front of me is To make a system Which compares The plot of any two movies And Gives me A similarity score Along with it The higher the similarity score The more similar the movie will be And we can recommend it to the user So this is our Next plan of action It seems simple To listen to the plan But it is not that easy To execute it Because the challenge in front of you Is that you have to Compare two pieces of text Now if you ask any computer science student Then between two pieces of text The semantic meaning Comparing them Is a very difficult NLP task But guess what After deep learning This problem is also solved So I hope you have heard About embeddings Embeddings is basically Technique With the help of which You can represent any piece of text Which has semantic meaning In the form of numbers So what you do is You pick up this piece of text And send it In a neural network And what this neural network does Is that it tries to understand The hidden meaning And once it understands Then it represents The meaning in the form Of numbers or vectors So you can see What you can see on the screen Is a vector It can be of any dimension It can be 512 It can be 784 or more But what is hidden in these numbers Is the meaning of this text So now what you will do That you will use Embedding To transform All of these text To vectors Or numbers So you can see This is the first movie's embedding vector This is the second movie's embedding vector This is the third movie's embedding vector And this is the fourth movie's embedding vector Now since we have Converted all our movie plots Into numbers So now it is easy to Get the correct answer How? I will tell you For a while, you can assume That all these vectors Are of 512 dimensions Means that each vector has 512 numbers Ok? Now what you can do That all these vectors Can be plotted Into a 512 dimensional coordinate system Right? Like this is your M4 This is your M3 This is your M2 This is your M1 Ok? So basically Each arrow here Is representing one of these vectors Now suppose someone asked you That M4 is a woman So tell me Which is the closest And most similar movie of a woman In your database So now what you will do That you will use your current vector And you will get the similarity Or you will get the angular distance So M4 has a lot of angular distance With M3 M4 has a lot of angular distance with M2 But M4 has Very less angular distance with M1 And the reason for the decrease of angular distance Means that In between these two movies The similarity is very high So you will say that The most similar movie of M4 is M1 Which is 3 idiots Ok? And this is how This entire technique will work Step by step if we talk So first of all you Calculated the plot of each movie Then you made the corresponding Embedding vectors of all the plots After that You calculated the similarities Between those embedding vectors Cosine similarity And the movie whose similarity is the highest And the movie whose similarities are the lowest Those are dissimilar movies And now you have A mechanism Which Is giving recommendations To the users On the similarity of the plots So think about it Now if I have the stories Of these four movies Then I can tell you very easily That these two movies will be similar And these two movies will be dissimilar Ok? So I hope you understood that How this kind of semantic meaning based Movie recommender system will work So conceptually we understood But when you will build this system Then you will face 3-4 problems Once I will list down Those problems in front of you Challenge no. 1 Will be To generate Embedding vectors You have Maybe Millions of movies data So for every movie You have to generate these embedding vectors This is your challenge no. 1 Challenge no. 2 Is storage Now obviously these embedding vectors You will generate You will generate it once And you will use it again and again To calculate the similarities So once you Generate these embedding vectors You will have to properly Store them So that you can retrieve them Whenever you need them The only problem is You don't store embedding vectors In normal relational databases Like MySQL or Oracle Because Once you store Embedding vectors in these types of databases Then you can't calculate Similarities between them Because these databases don't give you these features So basically you need A different type of database To store such embedding vectors So this is challenge no. 2 And challenge no. 3 is Semantic search What we have to do Is We have to go into these vectors And search which vector Is most similar To a given query vector So for this We have to find the cosine similarity But think about it Suppose I have to Find the most similar 5 movies of M1 And I have 10 lakh Embedding vectors So what I have to do Is I have to calculate The similarity of M1 With these 10 lakh Vectors Now think about it yourself If you do it one by one And calculate the similarity With every movie Vector by vector basis Then this is a computationally Very heavy task And it will take a lot of time So your application Will be very slow And the user's experience will be ruined So we have to Find a smart way That we don't have to Find a way And in very less number of Comparisons To find out which movie Is most similar Out of 10 lakh movies So I need intelligent Semantic search So these are the three biggest challenges That you will face When you go to make a system like this And guess what Who helps you In solving these three challenges The answer is Vector store Which is the topic of today's video So I know I have talked about this But I really wanted to Plant this idea very deep inside your mind That why Vector stores are super important So if you ever Want to build an application Where there are such requirements Then you use Vector stores To solve such problems So now that we know Why we need vector stores Let's move and discuss What exactly is a vector store And what are its core features And how a vector store solves these problems So here I have written a definition Of a vector store Let's read it Here it is written A vector store is a system Designed to store and retrieve Data represented as numerical vectors So if you are ever Building an application Where you have to Store and retrieve Vectors Then the system Which is used to handle such applications Is called a vector store Okay If you want to know the key features of a vector store Then there are four key features We will discuss one by one The first one is storage So the primary feature Of any vector store Is that it gives you The option to store Vectors You are building any kind of application If there are vectors Then you can store them With the help of vector stores It is written here Ensures that vectors and their associated metadata Are retained whether in memory For quick lookups Or on disk for durability And large scale use Let me show you the associated metadata So Suppose What we did here We had this database We generated Vectors from this plot So now we have to Store these vectors somewhere So we will store them in vector store But apart from storing these vectors You can store Other things in vector store Like if you want to store the movie's ID Or if you want to store the movie's name You can store that too So basically any metadata Related to that vector You can store it in vector store Okay Second point is written here That your vector stores Give you two storage options If you want You can store Vectors in memory Or On disk You can store on disk In memory means You can store in RAM And on disk means You can save it in your hard drive Or You can store it in any database So storing in RAM means If you close the application Then those vectors will be gone Hard drive or On disk saving means They will be persistent When you close the application and open it again So those vectors will be there So you have two types of options Okay So if you are making small application You will use in memory If you are making proper enterprise level application You will use on disk But you get these two options in storage When you talk about vector stores Okay Apart from that You get the option to search similarity Any vector store Gives you The option that All the vectors are there You can compare A query vector With all those vectors And generate a similarity score Okay You have this option With this you can Extract the most similar vector For a given query vector It is written here exactly It helps retrieve the vectors Most similar to a query vector Third The most interesting feature Is indexing So if you have read the databases RDBMS etc You might have read the concept of indexing Indexing is generally used To make the searching process fast Okay So you get the indexing concept In vector stores Okay What happens in indexing It is written here Or a method That enables fast Similarity searches on high dimensional Vectors Okay, how it works I will give you an example There are many types of indexing techniques But I will tell you a simple indexing technique So the challenge in front of us Is that in our vector store Let's say For now 10 lakh Vectors are stored And every vector Is 784 dimension Now I have a new query vector It also has 784 dimension And what we have to do We have to Remove that vector From these 10 lakh vectors Which is The closest or similar To this given vector Okay, now the problem in this Is that you have to Find out Which is the closest vector You have to Calculate the similarity With 10 lakh vectors Now this searching is linear And of order of n Which means I have to do 10 lakh computations To find out my answer And this is very very slow Right Because you are doing 10 lakh comparisons And taking out similarity scores So it will take a lot of time To find out the result So to solve this problem What you can do Is to use the concept of indexing So indexing Works in many ways I will give you a simple example Where we will take the help of clustering To solve this problem So what we will do Is that we will Cluster these 10 lakh vectors Using any clustering algorithm Which we have studied in the past Okay, suppose We have 10 clusters Formed And in each cluster Roughly 1 lakh Vectors are there Okay, so in a way I have 10 lakh vectors Organized into 10 clusters Okay, and in each cluster Roughly 1 lakh vectors are there Now what I will do All the vectors of these 10 clusters I will take out the average Of all the vectors So that I can get A centroid vector for each cluster Okay, so now I have 10 centroid vectors Now as soon as a new query vector comes What I will do I will calculate the similarity Of this query vector With these 10 centroid vectors Okay, and I will try to find out Which centroid vector Is the most similar To our query vector With the same centroid vector Now after comparing 10 vectors I got to know That the centroid of C3, the cluster 3 Is the most similar To my given vector So now what I will do I will ignore the rest of the clusters And I will only Focus on cluster 3 Which has around 1 lakh vectors Now I will Calculate the similarity Of my query vector With these 1 lakh vectors And the most similar Vector that comes out in this Is my answer Now what is the benefit of this whole process That instead of doing 10 lakh comparisons I have to do 10 more comparisons These 10 comparisons Are your centroid ones So instead of 10 lakh You had to do only 1 lakh comparison But roughly you Extracted your best result So this technique is called Indexing, I told you one way Other than this there are many other ways To do the same thing Like there is a very famous technique Approximate nearest neighbour look up So again we are not going Because this is a very famous research topic But you get the idea How vector stores Smartly implement these algorithms So that the similarity searches That you are doing Can be done very fast And your application can be done very fast So this is your third feature And the fourth feature Is a very regular feature That with this you can perform All the crud applications Which you can perform with any other database This means that you can easily Add new vectors in any vector store You can retrieve You can update existing vectors And You can delete outdated vectors Right? This same feature is given to you by other databases Vector store also gives you this feature So in nutshell the 4 most important features That you get with any vector store Is storage Similarity search feature Indexing for fast similarity searches Similarity searches and crud operations Right? I hope you are getting the idea What are vector stores And where they are useful Where they are useful So here I have added this point What are the use cases We have seen the recommender system Apart from this If you want to perform a semantic search Between two vectors in any application You can use vector store Rag which we are reading We will cover it in detail in the next video So in rag Vector stores are used a lot And finally if you want to Search for image or any type of multimedia You can use vector stores In nutshell If your application In any shape and form Is using vectors Is storing them Is retrieving them You can use vector stores And they will perform much much better In traditional relational databases Because traditional relational databases Are not great At storing vectors And retrieving vectors So I hope I was able to give you an overview Of what are vector stores Exactly Now how vector stores are used In the blank chain Before reading this I want to discuss a small topic With you And that is vector store What is vector database If you go to youtube Or read anywhere You will see these two terms interchangeably Some will call it vector store Some will call the same thing Vector database So I found it a little confusing When I started reading all this So I just want to clarify This a little in front of you So that you can understand things a little more Okay So the biggest difference Between these two concepts But the biggest difference Between vector store and vector database Is that You can consider vector store As a system Which basically Gives you two main tasks One Gives you the storage option Where you can store vectors And second Gives you the retrieval option Where you Basically Can perform semantic search on these vectors Okay If you are getting these two features Then you can call this system vector store But If you Add some more features here Add features which Generally you get to see in databases Like Distributed architecture With the help of which You can scale your system Or you can talk about Backup and restore Option That if tomorrow Data is lost, I have already made a backup Or you can talk about Asset transaction Which again Is a feature which you can see in Relational databases If you talk about concurrency control That multiple users Can communicate with this Vector database And lastly Authentication type feature That you have kept a security Behind the system So that no one can access it So if you In a vector store Add these database Like features Then this overall system Is called Vector database So basically vector store Plus database like features Is equal to vector database That's the main difference Here I have written That vector store Typically refers to a lightweight Library or service That focuses on storing vectors And performing similarity search This may not Include traditional database Features like transactions Rich query language like SQL And role based access control Authentication And it is ideal for prototyping And smaller scale applications This type of vector store Has a very famous example FICE Which is a Facebook library It's a vector store And if you talk about vector databases So vector databases Are a full fledged database system Designed to store And query vectors As I told you It offers additional database like features Distributed architecture Durability and persistence Where you can backup and restore Metadata handling Potential for acid or near acid Guarantees Acid transaction if you know You will understand these points And authentication and authorization for more advanced security So these vector databases Are used in production environments Where you need significant scaling Or dealing with big data sets So these types of vector databases Like Melvus, Quadrant VV8 Another very famous Pinecone So these software Are called vector databases By the way if you want to remember One thing A vector database is effectively A vector store With extra database features So in nutshell Every vector database Is a vector store Every vector database Is a vector store Every vector database Is after all A vector store but its opposite is not true Every vector store Is not a vector database Because it is not necessary That every vector store has Database like features So I hope I was able to clarify This point And hopefully you will not have confusion in the future So now that we understand The fundamentals of vector stores Now we will discuss How vector stores are implemented In the Lank chain So the whole story is When the Lank chain was made as a framework So its creators Had understood this very early on That in the future When LLM based applications will be made So there Will be a lot of work with embedding vectors Which is correct So if you make any application There you work with vector embeddings And obviously If you work with vector embeddings Then vector databases And vector stores will also appear in the picture So that is why in the Lank chain Early on There is a lot of support for different Type of vector stores Here you will get to see Components for all the famous vector stores For all of them For the suffiars For the suffiars, for the pinecones Chroma, quadrant, VV8 All these famous vector stores Which exist in the market Around all of them You will get wrappers in the Lank chain Now the main Funda is that Lank chain has developed These wrappers in this way That all these Wrappers of different vector stores They share the same Method signatures The name of these functions is the same And you can apply this Same function to the pinecones And the same function To the quadrants or to the VV8 You can apply So basically Lank chain has made some set of methods Which are common for every vector store Like from documents Or from text With the help of this you can create a vector store Add documents With the help of add text You can add new vectors In that vector store With the help of similarity search You can conduct semantic search And besides this There are also options to do Metadata based filtering The core philosophy is That if you build an application In which your vector store was Let's say 5S And You decided not to use 5S Let's say you want to use pinecones Because my application is a little big So you can Replace 5S With pinecones And you don't have to Change anything in your existing code Because These same functions Will work in pinecones In the same way as You were doing in 5S So basic funda is That in Lank chain You will get built in components And with the help of a common interface All the components have been designed So that tomorrow You can remove one and bring the other Then also you don't have to Make any major changes in your code So now what we are going to do We have a Very famous vector store Called Chroma With the help of that we will understand How you can create vector stores in Lank chain Add new documents Remove old documents How you can search for new documents We will see all this with the help of ChromaDB So guys Let's talk about ChromaDB So Chroma is a Lightweight open source vector database That is specially friendly For local development And small to medium scale Production needs So Chroma is one of the famous Vector databases The biggest feature of Chroma is That it is a database So it is a vector database And you will get many features Which are in a normal Vector database But at the same time it is also Very lightweight unlike some other Vector databases like Pyncon etc So in that sense You can say that Chroma can come Between A vector store And a vector database So Chroma is not a Full fledged vector database Because it is lightweight And it is not a simple Vector store because There are some database features So in that sense it will give you Both the same flavor If you want to understand its hierarchy How data is organized So at the top level of ChromaDB Is Tenant Which you can understand is a user A user or an organization Or a team Which comes at the top level Now this user can create Multiple databases And in one database You can create multiple Collections so you can understand Collection as a table If you have worked with RDBMS So the table in RDBMS Is equivalent to Collection in Chroma And in every collection What you can do is You can store documents Now there are two things in document One is Embedding vector And the other is Metadata about that vector So in this way ChromaDB is All organized So now I will give you A little flavor that with the help of Langchain you can work with ChromaDB How you can create a new collection How you can add New documents in that new collection How you can update existing documents How you can delete And most important How you can perform semantic search And we will cover a couple of topics Like how to Filter on the base of metadata So on the whole You will get a lot of ideas And once you understand ChromaDB You can easily switch it And work with FIAS and PINECON Basic concept is Same So now I will tell you how you can work with Langchain with ChromaDB So first of all We will write all the code in Google Colab You must have noticed that We were writing all the code in VS code But for this particular video I thought Google Colab is a better option So first of all you have to Install some libraries And I have written the name of all the libraries You have to simply run this cell As soon as you run this cell All the libraries will be installed And the rest of the code will work easily First of all we are importing These two libraries So we are importing Langchain underscore openai Embeddings And Langchain dot vector store We are importing Chroma You can import any other vector store In the same way Like FIAS, PINECON, VV8 You can import them in the same way Ok Now first of all I am making Document Objects I have told you about document objects A couple of videos ago How Langchain Uses document object format To handle textual data The biggest feature of document objects Is that it has two things First is page content Where you store your actual text And second is metadata Where you store text related Metadata So I am making five documents Each document is about a cricket player Like Virat Kohli, Rohit Sharma, MS Dhoni etc. And in each player's metadata I have written the name of the IPL team Of that player Ok So these are my five documents And now what I am doing I am forming a list In which I am putting all my document objects And I am calling this list DOGS Now the main work starts from here Here you are forming your vector store So you are making a chroma object And here you have to tell A couple of things First you have to tell What is your embedding function So it means When you insert document objects Inside your database Before that you have to convert Your documents into embeddings What is the role of converting them into embeddings In our case It would be OpenAI embeddings Second you have to tell Where you want to store your documents In the form of vectors Where will they be stored So in our root folder We are making a folder called ChromaDB I will show you after a while Our entire data All our embedding vectors will be stored there And lastly As I told you You are forming collections here In ChromaDB So we are making a collection Which is called Sample So you can see a folder In Google colab So somehow this folder is not being created I will change its name To MyChromaDB Because I have already Run the code in the name of ChromaDB And I made a folder And deleted it May be there is some issue due to that So we are making a new directory And you can see it is made So currently you can see A SQLite 3 file The interesting thing about ChromaDB Is that the vector database Which you are storing Is stored in SQLite 3 Which means you can Download it and run it In any SQL client Although you won't need it But I am telling you So in this step we have created our vector store Now we will use this vector store And perform different operations Like adding documents in vector store Updating, deleting Searching, doing all these operations I will show you one by one So first of all I will show you How you can add documents in your vector store So Langchain gives you A simple function called add documents With the help of this you can Put all the documents in your vector store So here I have provided Docs, here is my docs It has 5 documents Now the interesting thing is When you run this code Not only your documents Are being added But also every document Has a unique ID assigned So that later you can Retrieve that document Now these IDs Are being generated by default But if you want you can Pass your own IDs So for now every document has A separate ID generated This is for Virat Kohli document Below that Rohit Sharma Below that MS Dhoni So if you see How many documents are there in vector DB So you have a function called get In this you just tell me What you want to see about that document Like I want to see embeddings Documents and metadata As soon as I run this code First of all I can see That I have 5 documents And their 5 IDs This is my first document's embedding This is my second document's embedding This is my third document's embedding And so on After that these are my documents In which I have 5 documents And here is the metadata of every document So this way you can see What is currently stored in your database Now let's come to the most interesting thing We will now do searching So for this There is a function called similarity search So you will call VectorStore.similaritySearch And here you have a parameter Called query So you can write whatever query you have So I have 5 players Virat Kohli, Rohit Sharma, MS Dhoni Jaspreet Bhoomra and Ravinder Jadeja And I have asked Who among these are a bowler And here you have One more parameter K K means how many Similar objects You want to show in the result So if I write K is equal to 1 So the most similar vector And its corresponding document I will see only that As soon as I run this I am getting only one document And that document is about Jaspreet Bhoomra Because in these 5 Jaspreet Bhoomra is our model That he can be called the most bowler But as soon as I write K is equal to 2 So now I am getting 2 documents One is Jaspreet Bhoomra And second is Ravinder Jadeja Because maybe in his text Allrounder is written That he can bat and bowl So its that simple To search in your vector db Simply call the similarity search function Provide your query And tell how many results you want to see How many top results you want to see There is one more function similar If you want to Take out similarity And also see a similarity score Then you have this function Similarity search with score You will run this And you will get the same result But you will see a score against every result The less the score The better Because it represents distance Less distance means closer And similar to vector Other than this Another interesting feature is If you want you can filter On Metadata Like suppose I want to filter out How many players are there in our 5 players So what I did I kept the key But in the filter keyword I sent Chennai Super Kings In the dictionary Now as soon as I run this code I am getting 2 documents First document is about MS Dhoni Because he plays with Chennai Super Kings And second document is Ravinder Jadeja Which again plays with Chennai Super Kings So in this way You can filter on Metadata basis And this is a very useful feature When I will make a project And I will show you how much it can help you Lastly We learnt to add documents Learned to view Learned to search Now see how to update the existing document So what I will do is I am making a new document About Virat Kohli In which I have added something else The Virat Kohli that was written earlier Is something else Virat Kohli is one of the most successful And consistent batsman in IPL history Virat Kohli the former captain of Royal Challenger Bangalore Is renowned for his Aggressive leadership and consistency This is basically a new document And I want to update the existing one So what I will do is I will call the update document function I will provide a document ID And here what will be the document ID Which is the document ID Of Virat Kohli This one So I will copy it And go down And here I will provide the document ID And other than that I will provide the updated document And I am running this code It is updated Let's view And you can see now The Virat Kohli document is Updated So this way you can update any existing document Lastly If you want You can delete any particular document Like I want to delete the Virat Kohli document So I have a function called Delete Where I can provide Any number of ID's For now I have to delete one document So I am providing one ID I will run it I will call get again And you can see now I have only 4 documents left And Rohit Sharma, MS Dhoni, Jaspreet Bumrah, Ravinder Jaday But Virat Kohli document is Out So guys this was a short
